Name: latent_mixed_precision_quantization
Title: Latent Sensitivity-Aware Mixed Precision Quantization for Low-Latency Inference
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging latent sensitivity analysis of neural network layers, we can develop a mixed-precision quantization framework that optimally minimizes inference latency while maintaining model accuracy, distinguishing it from existing methods that primarily focus on accuracy without explicit latency considerations.
Related Work: While current literature such as KVTuner and MixQuant explores mixed precision and sensitivity-based quantization, they predominantly address accuracy and computational efficiency without a dedicated focus on latency. This proposal distinctly aims to balance inference latency with sensitivity-aware quantization, creating a framework that prioritizes real-time performance in latency-critical applications.
Abstract: In the realm of deep learning, achieving efficient inference is paramount, especially for resource-constrained environments. This proposal introduces a novel Latent Sensitivity-Aware Mixed Precision Quantization (LSAMPQ) framework, which leverages latent sensitivity analysis of neural network layers to optimize mixed-precision quantization. By systematically analyzing the sensitivity of each layer to quantization errors, LSAMPQ determines the optimal bit-width configurations that minimize inference latency while preserving accuracy. This framework distinguishes itself from existing methods by explicitly incorporating latency targets into the quantization process, allowing for a more robust model deployment in real-time applications. We will demonstrate the effectiveness of LSAMPQ through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, evaluating the trade-offs between latency and accuracy across various architectures. Our approach aims to set a new standard for mixed-precision quantization techniques by integrating latency as a critical performance metric.
Experiments: Conduct a series of ablation studies on standard datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10) using popular architectures (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet) to evaluate the performance of LSAMPQ against existing quantization methods.
Implement a layer sensitivity analysis to identify the impact of bit-width configurations on both latency and accuracy, measuring inference time and model performance metrics.
Evaluate the proposed framework under varying hardware constraints (e.g., different GPUs) to assess its adaptability and performance consistency.
Use metrics such as throughput, accuracy, and latency to construct a Pareto front to visualize the trade-offs and derive optimal configurations for deployment.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method may not generalize well to all network architectures, particularly those with unique or complex structures.
Latency measurements can vary significantly based on hardware and implementation nuances, potentially affecting reproducibility.
Identifying and quantifying sensitivity may introduce additional computational overhead, which could offset some latency gains.
